narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've poured into your undergrad years and LSAT prep—it's tough to hear that your law school application didn't make the cut because our model sees a 50.3% chance you might struggle to pass the bar exam down the line. Picture it like this: the model looks at patterns from thousands of students who've walked this path before, and for you, it zeroed in first on your Year 1 grades, placing you in the 3rd decile at school—that early footing was the biggest signal pulling toward potential challenges ahead. Layered on top was your LSAT score of 35, which, while solid for many, stood out as the second strongest factor tilting the prediction toward failure in the model's view of bar success stories. Your middle family income bracket added another layer, as it often correlates with fewer resources for the intense prep that top passers lean on. Even your Year 3 decile of 2 nudged things further in that direction, showing the model a trajectory that didn't quite climb as high as it needed to. Your white background, undergraduate GPA of 3.4, being female, and full-time study plans all played supporting roles, each whispering "higher risk" based on historical patterns the model has learned. It's not that any one thing defines you—it's this combination painting a picture of someone who might face steeper odds without extra support. The good news? You're not the prediction; with targeted steps like bolstering study habits or seeking mentorship, you can rewrite this story. You've already shown grit getting a 3.4 UGPA—channel that into bridging those early gaps, and doors will open wider. Hang in there; this is just one chapter, and your determination can turn the page.
